name: rds
context: Create and manage Amazon RDS managed database instances
info: Amazon RDS (Relational Database Service) provides managed relational databases in the cloud. RDS handles provisioning, patching, backups, recovery, and scaling — you focus on your application and queries. Supported engines include MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, and Amazon Aurora (MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible with up to 5x performance). RDS instances run in a VPC with security group access control. Automated backups enable point-in-time recovery. Read replicas provide horizontal read scaling. The AWS SDK v3 `@aws-sdk/client-rds` package provides CreateDBInstance, DescribeDBInstances, and other management commands. Use [[aws/cloudformation]] to provision RDS instances as infrastructure-as-code and [[aws/iam]] for IAM database authentication.
body:
# Amazon RDS

RDS is AWS's managed relational database service. It handles the heavy lifting of database administration — provisioning, patching, backups, failover, and scaling — so you can focus on your schema and queries. Unlike running a database on [[aws/ec2]], RDS automates operational tasks.

## Create an Instance

```javascript
import { RDSClient, CreateDBInstanceCommand } from "@aws-sdk/client-rds";

const rds = new RDSClient({ region: process.env.AWS_REGION });

await rds.send(new CreateDBInstanceCommand({
  DBInstanceIdentifier: "my-database",
  Engine: "postgres",
  EngineVersion: "16.1",
  DBInstanceClass: "db.t3.micro",
  AllocatedStorage: 20,
  MasterUsername: "admin",
  MasterUserPassword: "SecurePassword123!", // Use Secrets Manager in production
  DBName: "myapp",
  VpcSecurityGroupIds: ["sg-0123456789abcdef0"],
  PubliclyAccessible: false,
  StorageEncrypted: true,
  BackupRetentionPeriod: 7,
}));
```

Set `PubliclyAccessible: false` and use VPC security groups to restrict access. Store credentials in AWS Secrets Manager, not in code. See [[aws/iam#best-practices]] for credential management.

## Database Engines

RDS supports multiple engines — choose based on your application needs:

| Engine | Version | Use Case |
|--------|---------|----------|
| PostgreSQL | 13-16 | General purpose, advanced features, JSON support |
| MySQL | 8.0 | Widely adopted, good for web applications |
| MariaDB | 10.6-10.11 | MySQL-compatible, community driven |
| Aurora PostgreSQL | Compatible with PG 13-16 | 3-5x PostgreSQL performance, auto-scaling storage |
| Aurora MySQL | Compatible with MySQL 8.0 | 5x MySQL performance, multi-master |
| Oracle | 19c, 21c | Enterprise workloads, Oracle-specific features |
| SQL Server | 2019, 2022 | Windows/.NET ecosystems |

Aurora is recommended for production workloads requiring high performance and availability. For standard PostgreSQL workflows and query patterns, see [[postgres]].

## Backups and Snapshots

RDS provides two backup mechanisms:

- **Automated backups** — daily snapshots + transaction logs. Enable point-in-time recovery (PITR) to restore to any second within the retention period (1-35 days).
- **Manual snapshots** — on-demand snapshots that persist until you delete them. Use before major changes.

```javascript
import { CreateDBSnapshotCommand } from "@aws-sdk/client-rds";

await rds.send(new CreateDBSnapshotCommand({
  DBSnapshotIdentifier: "my-database-pre-migration",
  DBInstanceIdentifier: "my-database",
}));
```

## Read Replicas

Read replicas provide horizontal scaling for read-heavy workloads:

- Create up to 5 read replicas per source instance (15 for Aurora)
- Replicas can be in different availability zones or regions
- Applications direct read queries to replicas, write queries to the primary
- Replicas use asynchronous replication — slight lag is possible
- Promote a replica to a standalone instance for disaster recovery

Read replicas reduce load on the primary instance and improve read throughput. For database design patterns and query optimization, see [[database]].

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID` | IAM access key for the RDS client |
| `AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` | IAM secret key for the RDS client |
| `AWS_REGION` | Target region for the RDS instance |

sections.title: Create a database instance
sections.body: Use `CreateDBInstanceCommand` with engine, instance class, storage, and credentials. Choose `db.t3.micro` for free tier. Set `PubliclyAccessible: false` and restrict via security groups. Enable `StorageEncrypted: true` for encryption at rest. Store master credentials in AWS Secrets Manager. Use [[aws/cloudformation]] to provision RDS instances declaratively.

sections.title: Database engines
sections.body: PostgreSQL and MySQL are the most common open-source engines. Aurora (PostgreSQL/MySQL-compatible) offers 3-5x performance with auto-scaling storage. Oracle and SQL Server for enterprise. Choose based on application requirements, team expertise, and cost. For PostgreSQL-specific features and query patterns, see [[postgres]].

sections.title: Backups and snapshots
sections.body: Automated backups run daily with transaction log archiving for point-in-time recovery (PITR). Retention period 1-35 days. Manual snapshots persist indefinitely — take before migrations or major changes. Restore to a new instance from any backup or snapshot. Cross-region snapshot copy for disaster recovery.

sections.title: Read replicas
sections.body: Create read replicas with `CreateDBInstanceReadReplicaCommand`. Route read queries to replicas, writes to primary. Up to 5 replicas per instance (15 for Aurora). Cross-AZ and cross-region replicas available. Asynchronous replication — monitor replica lag. Promote replicas for disaster recovery. For query optimization patterns, see [[database]].

links.context: IAM policies and database authentication control RDS access
EC2 instances connect to RDS within the same VPC
Provision RDS instances via CloudFormation templates
Use IAM authentication instead of database passwords
Store database credentials in Secrets Manager
Security groups control network access to RDS

related.note: PostgreSQL knowledge is essential for RDS PostgreSQL and Aurora PostgreSQL engines
General database design patterns and query optimization apply to RDS
IAM credentials and policies are required for RDS management
EC2 is the alternative for self-managed databases with full OS control
CloudFormation is the recommended way to provision RDS in production

scripts.name: Manage RDS

AWS_REGION.description: AWS region for the RDS instance

dbName.description: Name of the initial database to create

depends.reason: IAM credentials with rds:CreateDBInstance permission are required
